caveats: a: Occupation data are at NAICS 315100 and include operations outside the narrowed contract-manufacturing lens.
a: Employment shares are not wage shares; management, business, design, and sales work carries above-average wages.
a: The estimate excludes benefits from robotics that cannot reasonably be attributed to AI-enabled substitution or avoidable hiring.
rho: USITC technology cases are illustrative innovators, not a representative adoption survey.
rho: The Auburn survey spans manufacturing sectors and may not reflect very small apparel plants.
rho: Implementation concerns labor capture only and excludes price, demand, and valuation effects.
e: No public data separate contract knitting mills from own-brand manufacturers at this code.
e: The dataset provides no defensible LMM firm count, so this eligibility share cannot be translated into a target count.
e: Some firms combine contract production and their own labels, requiring revenue-level classification.
s5: The owner-age source covers women owners across all manufacturing, not all owners in apparel knitting.
s5: Age 55 or older does not reveal retirement intent or timing.
s5: The missing dataset firm count prevents a defensible absolute estimate of transferable firms.
q: No public source measures retention of automation benefits for private US contract apparel knitters.
q: Yarn, freight, tariff, and mix changes can swamp labor-cost effects in invoice data.
q: Retention is likely lowest for standardized high-volume programs and highest for fast, customized, or technically difficult orders.
d5: BLS data are at NAICS 3151 and include business models excluded from the lens.
d5: Domestic output measures US supply, so import substitution can drive it below underlying US apparel demand.
d5: Trade policy, sourcing-country shifts, and tariff rules can move five-year demand sharply in either direction.
o: The estimate is inferred from physical production requirements rather than directly observed.
o: A highly automated plant still counts as an accountable operator; lower labor intensity is captured in other primitives.
o: Offshore substitution removes work from US lens firms even though a physical operator remains elsewhere.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B contract, private-label, replenishment, small-batch, and on-demand production, generally quoted per unit or production run with customer specifications, samples, purchase orders, quality requirements, and delivery commitments.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 315120 includes both own-account branded apparel producers and mills supplying outsourced knitting and garment production. Those models have different commercial retention and demand drivers, so the lens retains recurring contract/private-label manufacturers and excludes primarily own-brand retail economics.
lens.excluded_activities: Primarily own-brand apparel companies whose economics depend on retail merchandising and consumer demand rather than outsourced manufacturing, captive mills, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and operations without transferable production and customer systems.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market apparel knitting mills that repeatedly manufacture knit garments or knit fabric-and-garment combinations for external brands, designers, uniform programs, institutions, and private-label customers under contract, replenishment, or recurring production arrangements.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment and wages for four-digit NAICS 315100, not specifically the 315120 contract/private-label lens, and does not measure AI task exposure. I mapped high partial exposure to design, product development, quoting, order administration, scheduling, customer service, inventory, quality documentation, and visual inspection, while assigning low exposure to knitting-machine operation, maintenance, sewing, packing, and material movement. The result may overstate opportunity where whole-garment machinery and workflow software have already automated tasks, and understate it where custom development and inspection are unusually labor intensive.
caveats: Occupation data are at NAICS 315100 and include operations outside the narrowed contract-manufacturing lens.
Employment shares are not wage shares; management, business, design, and sales work carries above-average wages.
The estimate excludes benefits from robotics that cannot reasonably be attributed to AI-enabled substitution or avoidable hiring.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory at 10-15 US contract apparel knitting mills, separating currently automated from manual design, sampling, quoting, order-entry, planning, machine monitoring, inspection, sewing, maintenance, packing, and customer-service hours.
a.rationale: BLS reports 54.31% production employment, 14.40% maintenance, and 11.08% material-moving work, limiting software-only substitution. Office and administrative support is 7.80%, business and financial operations 3.65%, management 3.50%, design 1.67%, and sales 1.28%; these higher-paid functions plus inspection and production-planning tasks are more exposed. May 2024 data still show knitting-machine operators, mechanics, packers, sewing operators, and inspectors as the largest occupations.

d5.bridge: BLS measures real output for four-digit NAICS 3151, not the narrowed contract/private-label subset or a constant-quality service basket. The 2019-2025 output trend of -4.1% annually compounds to roughly 0.81 over five years, which anchors the base at 0.82. This may understate the retained lens if on-demand, short-run, customization, and supply-chain diversification outperform own-brand domestic production; it may overstate it if imports continue displacing even differentiated US contracts.
caveats: BLS data are at NAICS 3151 and include business models excluded from the lens.
Domestic output measures US supply, so import substitution can drive it below underlying US apparel demand.
Trade policy, sourcing-country shifts, and tariff rules can move five-year demand sharply in either direction.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price order and unit-volume histories from a panel of contract knitters, separated by replenishment, uniform, technical, luxury, startup, and on-demand programs, plus product-level import penetration, brand sourcing surveys, domestic capacity additions, and customer reshoring commitments.
d5.rationale: BLS reports that apparel knitting mill output fell 4.1% annually from 2019 through 2025 while hours fell 7.7% and productivity rose 3.9%. USTR describes decades of apparel-manufacturing decline and intense import sensitivity, while USITC identifies domestic advantages in customization, short lead times, smaller orders, and automation. The central case continues contraction but leaves upside for near-market programs.

caveats: No public data separate contract knitting mills from own-brand manufacturers at this code.
The dataset provides no defensible LMM firm count, so this eligibility share cannot be translated into a target count.
Some firms combine contract production and their own labels, requiring revenue-level classification.
e.change_evidence: A complete business census from D&B, Capital IQ, PitchBook, state manufacturing directories, and industry associations, coding every US 315120 firm by contract/private-label revenue share, recurring customer programs, captive status, normalized EBITDA band, and standalone management.
e.rationale: The official code covers establishments knitting apparel or knitting fabric and then manufacturing apparel, without separating contract producers from own-brand manufacturers. USITC documents domestic knitting and apparel-production services for startups and brands, including small-run and on-demand work, but branded producers and captive operations fall outside the narrowed outsourced-service lens. Eligibility is therefore plausibly substantial but not a majority with confidence.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) at ancestor 3151: labor compensation (L02) $356.7M / sectoral output (T30) $868.1M, year 2024

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $134,201 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 3151); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: The estimate is inferred from physical production requirements rather than directly observed.
A highly automated plant still counts as an accountable operator; lower labor intensity is captured in other primitives.
Offshore substitution removes work from US lens firms even though a physical operator remains elsewhere.
o.change_evidence: Customer and brand interviews on self-service design, owned knitting capacity, and supplier replacement; operating data from automated whole-garment factories showing which functions remain human- or operator-accountable; and evidence on platform-owned distributed production.
o.rationale: Year-five customers still require physical knit garments. AI and whole-garment automation can collapse design, sampling, and production labor, but an accountable manufacturer remains responsible for yarn and material procurement, machine programming and uptime, physical production, quality, fit, compliance, delivery, and remediation. Some design and ordering can become self-service, and customers can insource machines or source offshore, leaving a small non-lens residual.

caveats: No public source measures retention of automation benefits for private US contract apparel knitters.
Yarn, freight, tariff, and mix changes can swamp labor-cost effects in invoice data.
Retention is likely lowest for standardized high-volume programs and highest for fast, customized, or technically difficult orders.
q.change_evidence: Customer- and program-level gross-margin bridges before and after automation, including open-book costing, price-reset clauses, annual bid calendars, offshore quote comparisons, reorders, win/loss reasons, customer concentration, and separate economics for replenishment versus fashion runs.
q.rationale: Per-unit and per-run contract pricing lets mills initially retain production, design, and administrative savings. Apparel brands are sophisticated purchasers with offshore alternatives, frequent sourcing decisions, and strong pressure on unit cost, so renewal bids and price competition share benefits over time. Short lead time, domestic small-batch flexibility, customization, reliable fit, quality, and integrated knit-to-garment capability preserve some margin.

rho.bridge: USITC describes concrete but selective apparel automation, on-demand workflow, 3-D prototyping, whole-garment knitting, and persistent adoption barriers; Auburn surveys manufacturers across industries rather than LMM apparel knitters. I translate this evidence into the five-year share of exposed AI opportunity implemented after integration, quality, and change constraints. Apparel examples may overstate readiness because they feature innovators, while packaged design and order tools may diffuse faster than the older evidence suggests.
caveats: USITC technology cases are illustrative innovators, not a representative adoption survey.
The Auburn survey spans manufacturing sectors and may not reflect very small apparel plants.
Implementation concerns labor capture only and excludes price, demand, and valuation effects.
rho.change_evidence: Deployment cohorts from knitting-machine, PLM, ERP, vision-inspection, and digital-sampling vendors segmented to US plants under 250 employees, with implementation duration, failure rate, human-review requirements, uptime, and verified labor-hour reductions.
rho.rationale: The industry already demonstrates rapid whole-garment knitting, automated order-to-production workflows, virtual sampling, and defect-detection use cases, but USITC also reports that apparel automation lags because fabric is hard to control and import competition weakens investment economics. Auburn's 2024 survey found only 8% using AI and 58% at awareness or research. Five years supports substantial back-office, design, planning, and inspection implementation, not full plant autonomy.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 62.4% of women owners of manufacturing employer businesses were age 55 or older in 2020; it does not measure all apparel-knitting owners or completed control transfers. I use the age profile only as evidence of succession pressure and haircut it for owners who retain, transfer internally, close, or sell equipment without a continuing operation. Women-owned manufacturing may be older than the overall population, biasing upward, while founder dependence and industry contraction may turn succession into closure, also reducing qualifying transfers.
caveats: The owner-age source covers women owners across all manufacturing, not all owners in apparel knitting.
Age 55 or older does not reveal retirement intent or timing.
The missing dataset firm count prevents a defensible absolute estimate of transferable firms.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of NAICS 315120 transactions, closures, and failed sale mandates from PitchBook, Axial, GF Data, BizBuySell, apparel-industry brokers, UCC filings, and state records, matched to owner age, control status, buyer type, and post-transfer continuity.
s5.rationale: An older manufacturing-owner population supports meaningful five-year succession activity, but apparel knitting has a very small and contracting domestic footprint. A base just under one-quarter reflects possible strategic, family, or sponsor sales while excluding internal reorganizations, non-control investments, deaths without transferable operations, and liquidations.

principal_driver: Integrated digital design and whole-garment production create a credible path to faster, more customized domestic contract manufacturing.
principal_weakness: Long-running domestic output contraction and the missing LMM firm count make demand and acquisition availability unusually uncertain.

sources.supports: Reports 7,010 jobs and the detailed occupation mix: production 54.31%, maintenance 14.40%, material moving 11.08%, office support 7.80%, business/financial 3.65%, management 3.50%, design/media 1.67%, and sales 1.28%.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 315100

sources.supports: Lists the largest apparel-knitting-mill occupations in 2024, led by 1,350 knitting and weaving machine operators, 480 industrial machinery mechanics, 460 hand packers, 450 sewing machine operators, and 390 inspectors/testers.
sources.title: May 2024 OEWS Industry Chart Data

sources.supports: Documents apparel automation and barriers, including 3-D whole-garment knitting in 30 minutes or less, virtual sampling, order-to-delivery integration, design/planning connectivity, lagging automation due to fabric handling and import competition, and domestic advantages in customization, smaller orders, and short lead times.
sources.title: Textiles and Apparel: Made in USA... Again?

sources.supports: For NAICS 3151, reports 6,800 jobs in 2025 and annual 2019-2025 changes of 3.9% in labor productivity, -4.1% in real output, and -7.7% in hours worked.
sources.title: Labor productivity, unit labor costs, and related data, 2019-2025

sources.supports: USTR states that US apparel manufacturing declined steadily over decades, notes interest in domestic and near-buyer production, and describes textile and apparel as particularly sensitive to import competition.
sources.title: Adapting Trade Policy for Supply Chain Resilience

sources.supports: Using 2021 Annual Business Survey data for 2020, reports that 62.4% of women owners of manufacturing employer businesses were age 55 or older, a broad succession proxy.
sources.title: The Metamorphosis of Women Business Owners: A Focus on Age

sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments primarily engaged in knitting apparel or knitting fabric and then manufacturing apparel; the definition does not distinguish contract production from own-brand manufacturing.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 315120 Apparel Knitting Mills

sources.supports: Among 97 manufacturing respondents, 8% used AI, the implementing share rose 12 percentage points, 58% remained at awareness or research, and lack of business cases and workforce skills were leading barriers.
sources.title: ICAMS Smart Manufacturing Adoption Study 2024

title: Apparel Knitting Mills
